questions.question: What is the core idea of Support Vector Machines (SVM)?
options: Finding the hyperplane with the largest margin between classes
Minimizing the sum of squared errors
Building an ensemble of weak learners
questions.explanation: SVMs aim to find the optimal hyperplane that maximizes the margin between the closest points of different classes (support vectors).

questions.question: What are Support Vectors in an SVM?
options: The data points closest to the decision boundary that define the margin
questions.explanation: Support vectors are the instances located on the edge of the margin. They are the only points that affect the position of the decision boundary.

questions.question: What is the Margin in SVM?
options: The distance between the decision boundary and the nearest data points from each class
The distance between the two closest points from different classes
The total number of support vectors
The error rate of the classifier
questions.explanation: The margin is the distance between the decision boundary (hyperplane) and the nearest training instances from each class. SVM maximizes this margin.

questions.question: Why does SVM perform well on high-dimensional data?
options: It maximizes the margin, which reduces overfitting
It always uses all features equally
questions.explanation: By maximizing the margin, SVM creates a model with better generalization, which is particularly beneficial in high-dimensional spaces.

questions.question: What is Hard Margin Classification in SVM?
options: A classification that strictly requires all training instances to be correctly classified with no violations
A classification that allows some misclassification for better generalization
A classification that uses soft boundaries
questions.explanation: Hard margin SVM requires perfect separation with no instances inside the margin. It only works if the data is linearly separable.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of Hard Margin SVM?
options: It only works if the data is linearly separable and is sensitive to outliers
questions.explanation: Hard margin SVM fails when data is not perfectly separable. A single outlier can prevent finding a feasible hyperplane.

questions.question: What is Soft Margin Classification in SVM?
options: A method that allows some margin violations to achieve better generalization
A method that strictly separates all data points
A method that removes outliers before training
A method that only uses a subset of features
questions.explanation: Soft margin SVM introduces slack variables that allow some instances to be inside the margin or even misclassified, trading off perfect separation for better generalization.

questions.question: What does the C hyperparameter control in a Soft Margin SVM?
options: The trade-off between maximizing the margin and minimizing classification errors
questions.explanation: C is the regularization parameter. A smaller C creates a wider margin with more margin violations (more regularization). A larger C creates a narrower margin with fewer violations (less regularization).

questions.question: What happens when you decrease the C parameter in SVM?
options: The margin becomes wider and more instances are allowed inside it
The model becomes more complex and overfits
The model can only use linear kernels
questions.explanation: Smaller C = more regularization. The margin widens, more violations are tolerated, and the model generalizes better.

questions.question: What happens when you increase the C parameter in SVM?
options: The margin narrows and fewer violations are tolerated, potentially causing overfitting
The model automatically selects the best kernel
The model becomes less sensitive to outliers
questions.explanation: Larger C = less regularization. The margin narrows, fewer violations are allowed, and the model may overfit to training data.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn class implements a linear SVM classifier?
questions.explanation: LinearSVC is optimized for linear SVM classification and uses the liblinear library, making it faster than SVC with a linear kernel for large datasets.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn class is the general-purpose SVM classifier that supports different kernels?
questions.explanation: SVC (C-Support Vector Classification) supports linear, polynomial, RBF, and sigmoid kernels through the kernel parameter.

questions.question: What is the hinge loss function used for in SVM?
options: For classification by penalizing predictions that are not confident enough
questions.explanation: The hinge loss penalizes predictions that are on the wrong side of the margin. It encourages confident and correct classifications.

questions.question: What is the computational complexity of training a linear SVM with LinearSVC?
options: O(m) where m is the number of instances and n is the number of features
questions.explanation: LinearSVC using liblinear has approximately O(m × n) complexity, making it suitable for large datasets.

questions.question: What is the computational complexity of training an SVM with SVC and an RBF kernel?
questions.explanation: SVC with kernels has O(m² × n) to O(m³ × n) complexity, making it much slower and impractical for very large datasets.

questions.question: Why is feature scaling important for SVM?
options: It is not important for SVM
It reduces the number of support vectors
SVM is sensitive to feature scales; unscaled features can dominate the distance calculation
questions.explanation: SVM measures distances between instances. If one feature has a much larger scale than others, it dominates the distance metric, biasing the model.

questions.question: What is the Kernel Trick in SVM?
options: A method to reduce training time
A way to remove support vectors
A mathematical technique to implicitly map data to higher dimensions without computing the transformation explicitly
questions.explanation: The kernel trick computes the dot product in the high-dimensional space directly from the original space, avoiding expensive explicit transformations.

questions.question: Which kernel is the default in Scikit-Learn's SVC class?
questions.explanation: SVC uses the RBF kernel by default (kernel='rbf'), which is a Gaussian kernel that can handle nonlinear boundaries.

questions.question: What is the Polynomial Kernel in SVM?
options: A kernel that uses only linear features
A kernel that computes the similarity based on polynomial combinations of features
A kernel specifically for text data
questions.explanation: The polynomial kernel computes (γ × xᵀ × x' + r)ᵈ, allowing SVM to learn polynomial decision boundaries of degree d.

questions.question: What does the degree hyperparameter control in a Polynomial Kernel SVM?
options: The degree of the polynomial used to transform features
questions.explanation: The degree parameter controls the polynomial degree. Higher degrees allow more complex decision boundaries but increase overfitting risk.

questions.question: What is the Gaussian RBF (Radial Basis Function) Kernel?
options: A linear kernel for high-dimensional data
A kernel for text classification only
A kernel that measures similarity using a Gaussian function, allowing complex nonlinear boundaries
A kernel that performs feature selection
questions.explanation: The RBF kernel K(x, x') = exp(-γ × ||x - x'||²) measures similarity based on Euclidean distance, creating smooth decision boundaries.

questions.question: What does the gamma hyperparameter control in an RBF kernel SVM?
options: The number of dimensions in the transformed space
The influence radius of each training example
questions.explanation: Gamma (γ) controls how far the influence of a single training example reaches. High gamma = close influence (overfitting). Low gamma = far reach (underfitting).

questions.question: What happens when you increase gamma in an RBF kernel SVM?
options: Each example has a broader influence, causing smoother boundaries
Each example has a narrower influence, causing more complex and tighter boundaries
questions.explanation: High gamma means each instance has a narrow influence region, making the decision boundary more complex and potentially overfitting.

questions.question: What happens when you decrease gamma in an RBF kernel SVM?
options: The decision boundary becomes more complex
The model ignores all training data
Each example has a broader influence, creating smoother decision boundaries
questions.explanation: Low gamma means each instance has a wide influence, making the decision boundary smoother and more generalized.

questions.question: What is the similarity features technique for handling nonlinear data with a linear SVM?
options: Adding features based on similarity to landmark points using a kernel function
questions.explanation: Similarity features measure how similar each instance is to landmark points (e.g., using Gaussian similarity), allowing linear SVM to handle nonlinear problems.

questions.question: What is SVM Regression (SVR)?
options: A regression technique that tries to fit as many instances as possible within a margin around the predictions
questions.explanation: SVR fits a tube (margin) around the predicted values and tries to include as many training instances inside the tube as possible, similar to how SVM classification maximizes the margin.

questions.question: What does the epsilon hyperparameter control in SVR?
options: The width of the margin tube around the predictions
questions.explanation: Epsilon (ε) defines the width of the margin tube. Predictions within ε of the actual value incur no penalty.

questions.question: What happens when you increase epsilon in SVR?
options: The model becomes more sensitive to errors
The margin tube widens and more instances are considered correctly predicted
questions.explanation: A larger epsilon means a wider tube, so more instances fall inside the margin without penalty, resulting in a smoother model.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn class implements SVM Regression?
questions.explanation: SVR implements Support Vector Regression in Scikit-Learn.

questions.question: What is the difference between SVC and NuSVC in Scikit-Learn?
options: NuSVC only works with linear kernels
NuSVC uses a nu parameter that controls the upper bound on training errors and lower bound on support vectors
questions.explanation: NuSVC uses the nu parameter (0 to 1) instead of C. It directly bounds the fraction of margin errors and support vectors.

questions.question: When should you prefer LinearSVC over SVC with kernel='linear'?
options: When you have very few features
When you have a large dataset and want faster training
questions.explanation: LinearSVC is optimized for linear SVM and is much faster than SVC with a linear kernel, especially for large datasets.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using SGDClassifier with hinge loss over LinearSVC?
options: Online learning capability and memory efficiency for very large datasets
questions.explanation: SGDClassifier with hinge loss implements linear SVM using stochastic gradient descent, which supports online learning and handles out-of-core training.

questions.question: Can SVM output probability estimates directly?
options: No, but Scikit-Learn can calibrate probabilities using Platt scaling when probability=True
questions.explanation: Setting probability=True in SVC enables Platt scaling, which adds logistic regression on the SVM scores to estimate probabilities. This slows training.

questions.question: What is the effect of setting probability=True in Scikit-Learn's SVC?
options: It enables probability estimates but slows down training significantly
questions.explanation: probability=True uses 5-fold cross-validation to train a logistic regression model on the SVM scores (Platt scaling), which is computationally expensive.

questions.question: In which scenario would you choose an SVM over a Random Forest?
options: When you need feature importance scores
When you have categorical features only
When you need a very fast training algorithm
When you have high-dimensional data with clear margin separation
questions.explanation: SVM excels with high-dimensional data (e.g., text classification) where maximizing the margin leads to good generalization.

questions.question: What is a key disadvantage of SVM compared to Logistic Regression?
options: SVM is only for linear problems
SVM does not natively output probability estimates
questions.explanation: Unlike Logistic Regression which naturally outputs probabilities, SVM requires additional calibration (Platt scaling) for probability estimates.

questions.question: What does the coef_ attribute contain in a linear SVM?
options: The weights of the features in the decision function
questions.explanation: coef_ contains the feature weights (theta_1 to theta_n) that define the orientation of the decision hyperplane.

questions.question: What does the intercept_ attribute represent in an SVM?
options: The bias term (theta_0) of the decision function
questions.explanation: intercept_ is the bias term that shifts the decision boundary. The decision function is: sign(coef_ · x + intercept_).

questions.question: What is the decision_function in SVM?
options: A function that returns class probabilities
A function that returns the signed distance of each sample from the decision boundary
A function that returns the predicted class label
A function that returns the kernel matrix
questions.explanation: decision_function returns the distance from the hyperplane. Positive values are on one side, negative on the other. The magnitude indicates confidence.

questions.question: What is the main reason SVM with RBF kernel is slow on large datasets?
options: Its training complexity is between O(m²n) and O(m³n)
questions.explanation: The kernelized SVM's training time scales poorly with the number of instances, making it impractical for datasets with millions of samples.

questions.question: What preprocessing step is critical before training an SVM?
questions.explanation: Feature scaling is essential because SVM is distance-based. Unscaled features can dominate the optimization and lead to poor results.

questions.question: What is the effect of using a very large C value in SVM?
options: The model behaves like hard margin SVM, potentially overfitting
The number of support vectors increases
questions.explanation: Very large C means almost no margin violations are allowed, making the model sensitive to outliers and likely to overfit.

questions.question: What is the effect of using a very small C value in SVM?
options: The model allows many margin violations and may underfit
The decision boundary becomes more complex
questions.explanation: Very small C means heavy regularization. The margin is wide, many violations are tolerated, and the model may underfit.

questions.question: What is the kernel hyperparameter in Scikit-Learn's SVC?
options: The type of kernel function to use
questions.explanation: The kernel parameter selects the kernel function: 'linear', 'poly', 'rbf' (default), or 'sigmoid'.

questions.question: What does the 'scale' option for gamma in SVC mean?
options: Gamma uses 1 / (n_features * X.var()) as the default
questions.explanation: gamma='scale' uses 1 / (n_features × X.var()) as the default gamma value, providing a reasonable starting point.

questions.question: How does SVM handle multiclass classification in Scikit-Learn?
options: Using a single classifier for all classes
Using the One-vs-One strategy by default
Using the One-vs-Rest strategy by default
questions.explanation: Scikit-Learn's SVC uses the One-vs-One strategy by default for multiclass classification, training a classifier for each pair of classes.

questions.question: What is the Sigmoid Kernel in SVM?
options: A kernel based on the hyperbolic tangent function, similar to a neural network activation
A kernel for text data only
questions.explanation: The sigmoid kernel K(x, x') = tanh(γ × xᵀx' + r) is similar to a neural network activation function but is rarely used in practice.

questions.question: What is a practical approach for training SVM on very large datasets?
options: Use LinearSVC or SGDClassifier with hinge loss
Use a polynomial kernel with high degree
questions.explanation: For very large datasets, LinearSVC or SGDClassifier are preferred because of their linear complexity and efficiency.

questions.question: What is the relationship between SVM margin width and model complexity?
options: Wider margin means lower complexity and better generalization
Margin width does not affect complexity
Narrower margin always leads to better generalization
questions.explanation: A wider margin means a simpler decision boundary with better generalization. A narrow margin fits training data more closely, increasing complexity.

questions.question: When should you consider using kernel SVM over linear SVM?
options: When you have millions of instances
When your data is not linearly separable and you need nonlinear decision boundaries
When you need the fastest possible training
When feature scaling is not possible
questions.explanation: Kernel SVM (especially RBF) is useful when the data has complex, nonlinear patterns that a linear boundary cannot separate effectively.
